def: The rate of change of displacement of an object with direction.
ex: The car's {{velocity}} was 30 metres per second heading north.

def: The rate of change of velocity of an object over time.
ex: The {{acceleration}} due to gravity is about 9.81 metres per second squared.

def: A push or pull that can change an object's motion or shape.
ex: A net {{force}} acting on a body produces acceleration.

def: A measure of the amount of matter contained in an object.
ex: The {{mass}} of the object stays constant regardless of location.

def: The product of an object's mass and its velocity vector.
ex: The total {{momentum}} of the system is conserved during collisions.

def: The capacity of a system to do work or produce change.
ex: {{Energy}} can neither be created nor destroyed, only transformed.

def: The energy an object possesses due to its motion.
ex: A moving vehicle has {{kinetic energy}} proportional to velocity squared.

def: Stored energy held by an object due to its position.
ex: The raised weight stores gravitational {{potential energy}} equal to mgh.

def: The attractive force between masses, pulling objects toward each other.
ex: Earth's {{gravity}} gives falling objects a downward acceleration.

def: A force resisting relative motion between two surfaces in contact.
ex: {{Friction}} between the tyres and road slows the car down.

def: Energy transferred when a force moves an object through distance.
ex: The {{work}} done equals force multiplied by displacement along it.

def: The rate at which work is done or energy transferred.
ex: The engine's {{power}} output is measured in watts.

def: The tendency of an object to resist changes in motion.
ex: Because of {{inertia}}, passengers lurch forward when the bus brakes.

def: The straight-line distance and direction from start to finish.
ex: The runner's {{displacement}} was zero after a complete lap.

def: The scalar rate at which an object covers distance.
ex: The car's average {{speed}} on the motorway was ninety kilometres per hour.

def: The gravitational force acting on an object's mass.
ex: An astronaut's {{weight}} on the Moon is far less than on Earth.

def: A region where a mass experiences a gravitational force.
ex: The {{gravitational field}} strength near Earth is about 9.8 newtons per kilogram.

def: Motion under gravity alone, with no other force acting.
ex: During {{free fall}}, all objects accelerate at the same rate.

def: The constant maximum speed reached when drag balances weight.
ex: A skydiver reaches {{terminal velocity}} when air resistance equals gravity.

def: The net force directing an object toward the centre of circular motion.
ex: The {{centripetal force}} keeps the satellite in a circular orbit.

def: The turning effect of a force about a pivot point.
ex: A longer spanner applies greater {{torque}} to loosen the bolt.

def: A state where all forces and moments on a body balance.
ex: The beam remains in {{equilibrium}} when opposing moments are equal.

def: A collision conserving both kinetic energy and total momentum.
ex: In an {{elastic collision}}, the balls bounce apart without energy loss.

def: A collision where kinetic energy is not conserved but momentum is.
ex: In an {{inelastic collision}}, the objects deform and lose kinetic energy.

def: The curved path of an object thrown under gravity's influence.
ex: The cannonball's {{projectile motion}} traced a parabolic arc.

def: The total scalar length of the path travelled by an object.
ex: The total {{distance}} covered exceeded the straight-line displacement.

def: A quantity fully described by magnitude alone, without direction.
ex: Temperature is a {{scalar}}, whereas velocity is a vector.

def: A quantity having both magnitude and a specific direction.
ex: Force is a {{vector}} because it has both size and direction.

def: The SI unit of force, symbol N, equal to kg·m/s².
ex: A force of one {{newton}} accelerates one kilogram at one metre per second squared.

def: The SI unit of energy and work, symbol J.
ex: Lifting the book one metre required about ten {{joule}} of work.

def: The SI unit of power, equal to one joule per second.
ex: The motor consumes five hundred {{watt}} at full load.

def: The size or numerical value of a physical quantity.
ex: The {{magnitude}} of the force was twenty newtons.

def: The single force representing the combined effect of several forces.
ex: The {{resultant force}} determines the object's acceleration direction.

def: The support force acting perpendicular to a contact surface.
ex: The table exerts a {{normal force}} balancing the box's weight.

def: The pulling force transmitted along a string, rope, or cable.
ex: The {{tension}} in the rope supported the hanging mass.

def: A force or state that pushes a material inward, shortening it.
ex: The column experienced {{compression}} under the heavy roof load.

def: The resistive force opposing motion through a fluid or air.
ex: Air {{drag}} increases sharply with the vehicle's speed.

def: The forward driving force produced by an engine or propulsion.
ex: The rocket's {{thrust}} exceeded its weight, so it lifted off.

def: The stiffness of a spring relating force to extension.
ex: A stiff spring has a large {{spring constant}} in Hooke's law.

def: A material's ability to return to its original shape.
ex: The rubber band's {{elasticity}} lets it snap back after stretching.

def: The maximum displacement of an oscillation from equilibrium.
ex: The pendulum swung with decreasing {{amplitude}} due to damping.

def: A repeated back-and-forth motion about an equilibrium position.
ex: The mass on the spring underwent regular {{oscillation}}.

def: The time taken for one complete cycle of oscillation.
ex: The pendulum's {{period}} depends on its length and gravity.

def: The number of complete cycles occurring per unit time.
ex: The wave's {{frequency}} was measured in hertz.

def: The rate of change of angular displacement during rotation.
ex: The flywheel's {{angular velocity}} increased as it spun up.

def: The point where a body's entire mass appears concentrated.
ex: The object balances perfectly at its {{centre of mass}}.

def: Motion at constant velocity with zero net force acting.
ex: In {{uniform motion}}, the object covers equal distances in equal times.

def: A decrease in speed; acceleration opposite to the motion.
ex: Hard braking caused a rapid {{deceleration}} of the train.

def: The universal constant G in Newton's law of gravitation.
ex: The {{gravitational constant}} appears in the equation for gravitational force.

def: The curved path of a body around a massive object.
ex: The satellite completed one {{orbit}} of Earth every ninety minutes.

def: The product of force and the time it acts, changing momentum.
ex: A large {{impulse}} is delivered when the bat strikes the ball.

def: The mass contained per unit volume of a substance.
ex: Lead has a much higher {{density}} than aluminium.

def: The force exerted perpendicularly per unit area of surface.
ex: The {{pressure}} increases as the gas is compressed into a smaller volume.

def: Stored energy due to an object's height in a gravitational field.
ex: Water at the dam's top holds large {{gravitational potential energy}}.

def: The ratio relating frictional force to the normal force.
ex: Ice has a very low {{coefficient of friction}} between surfaces.

def: The principle that total energy in a closed system stays constant.
ex: By {{conservation of energy}}, potential energy converts into kinetic energy.

def: Total momentum of an isolated system remains constant over time.
ex: The {{conservation of momentum}} explains recoil when a gun fires.

def: The path followed by a moving object through space.
ex: The rocket's {{trajectory}} curved under the pull of gravity.

def: Movement of a body turning about a fixed axis.
ex: The spinning wheel is an example of {{rotational motion}}.

def: The rate of change of angular velocity over time.
ex: The motor produced a steady {{angular acceleration}} of the disc.

def: A force pushing a displaced object back toward equilibrium.
ex: The spring's {{restoring force}} pulls the mass back to centre.

def: Oscillation where restoring force is proportional to displacement.
ex: A mass on a spring performs {{simple harmonic motion}}.

def: A suspended mass that swings freely under gravity.
ex: The clock's {{pendulum}} swings with a constant period.

def: The resistive contact force opposing an object's sliding motion.
ex: The {{friction force}} eventually brought the sliding block to rest.

def: The constant acceleration a falling body gains from gravity.
ex: The {{acceleration due to gravity}} on Earth is about 9.81 metres per second squared.

def: The turning effect of a force about a point.
ex: The {{moment}} of a force equals force times perpendicular distance.

def: A rigid bar pivoting about a point to multiply force.
ex: A {{lever}} lets a small effort lift a heavy load.

def: The fixed pivot point about which a lever turns.
ex: Moving the {{fulcrum}} nearer the load reduces the effort needed.

def: The ratio of output force to input force in a machine.
ex: A pulley system gives a large {{mechanical advantage}} when lifting.

def: The ratio of useful energy output to total energy input.
ex: No real machine reaches a hundred percent {{efficiency}}.

def: The apparent outward force felt in a rotating reference frame.
ex: The {{centrifugal force}} pushes passengers outward on a sharp bend.

def: A region assigning an acceleration vector to each point.
ex: Near a planet, the {{acceleration field}} points toward its centre.

def: Motion where every point of a body moves identically.
ex: A sliding block undergoes pure {{translational motion}}.

def: The equal and opposite force paired with an action.
ex: By Newton's third law, each action has a {{reaction force}}.

def: The SI base unit of mass, symbol kg.
ex: The block had a mass of two {{kilogram}}.

def: The gradual loss of oscillation energy that reduces amplitude.
ex: {{Damping}} caused the pendulum's swings to shrink over time.

def: A physical property of matter causing it to experience force in an electric field.
ex: Protons carry a positive {{electric charge}} equal in size to an electron's.

def: A region around a charge where another charge experiences an electric force.
ex: The {{electric field}} points away from a positive point charge.

def: A region where a magnetic force acts on moving charges or magnetic materials.
ex: A compass needle aligns with the Earth's {{magnetic field}}.

def: The rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor, measured in amperes.
ex: The {{electric current}} increases when the voltage across the resistor rises.

def: The energy transferred per unit charge between two points in a circuit.
ex: A higher {{voltage}} drives a larger current through the lamp.

def: The opposition a component offers to the flow of electric current.
ex: A thin wire has greater {{resistance}} than a thick one.

def: A circuit component designed to provide a specific electrical resistance.
ex: The {{resistor}} limits the current flowing into the LED.

def: The rate at which electrical energy is transferred, measured in watts.
ex: The {{electric power}} equals the current multiplied by the voltage.

def: A material that allows electric charge to flow through it easily.
ex: Copper is an excellent {{conductor}} used widely in wiring.

def: A material that strongly resists the flow of electric current.
ex: Rubber acts as an {{insulator}} around the copper cable.

def: A material whose conductivity lies between that of conductors and insulators.
ex: Silicon is the {{semiconductor}} used in most computer chips.

def: A device that stores electric charge and energy in an electric field.
ex: The {{capacitor}} smooths the voltage in the power supply.

def: A closed path around which an electric current can flow.
ex: The lamp lights only when the {{circuit}} is complete.

def: A circuit in which components are connected one after another in sequence.
ex: In a {{series circuit}}, the same current passes through every component.

def: A circuit where components are connected across common points, forming separate branches.
ex: In a {{parallel circuit}}, each branch receives the full supply voltage.

def: The energy a source gives per unit charge driving current around a circuit.
ex: A battery's {{electromotive force}} equals its terminal voltage with no current.

def: The work done moving unit charge between two points in a circuit.
ex: A voltmeter measures the {{potential difference}} across the bulb.
ph: measured in volts, across a component

def: The SI unit of electric current, equal to one coulomb per second.
ex: The fuse blows if the current exceeds thirteen {{ampere}}.

def: The SI unit of electric charge, equal to one ampere-second.
ex: One {{coulomb}} of charge flows each second at one ampere.

def: The SI unit of electrical resistance, symbolised by the Greek omega.
ex: The resistor is rated at two hundred and twenty {{ohm}}.

def: An accumulation of electric charge on the surface of an object.
ex: Rubbing a balloon builds up {{static electricity}} that lifts hair.

def: A negatively charged subatomic particle that carries electric current in metals.
ex: Free {{electron}} movement produces the current in a copper wire.

def: The generation of voltage by a changing magnetic field near a conductor.
ex: {{Electromagnetic induction}} lets a generator convert motion into electricity.

def: The total magnetic field passing through a given surface area.
ex: Increasing the coil area raises the {{magnetic flux}} through it.

def: A magnet produced by passing electric current through a coil of wire.
ex: The {{electromagnet}} in the crane lifts heavy scrap steel.

def: A long coil of wire producing a uniform magnetic field when carrying current.
ex: The {{solenoid}} generates a strong field along its central axis.

def: A region of a magnet where the magnetic force appears strongest.
ex: Like {{magnetic pole}} ends repel, while opposite ones attract.

def: A device that changes the voltage of an alternating current supply.
ex: A step-up {{transformer}} raises voltage for efficient power transmission.

def: A machine that converts mechanical energy into electrical energy by induction.
ex: The wind turbine drives a {{generator}} that produces electricity.

def: A device that converts electrical energy into rotational mechanical motion.
ex: The {{electric motor}} spins the fan blades when switched on.

def: An electric current that periodically reverses its direction of flow.
ex: Mains electricity is supplied as {{alternating current}} at fifty hertz.

def: An electric current that flows steadily in one direction only.
ex: A battery supplies {{direct current}} to the small torch.

def: A disturbance that transfers energy through a medium or space.
ex: A water {{wave}} carries energy across the surface of the pond.

def: The distance between two successive corresponding points on a wave.
ex: Red light has a longer {{wavelength}} than blue light.

def: The SI unit of frequency, equal to one cycle per second.
ex: The tuning fork vibrates at four hundred and forty {{hertz}}.
ph: unit of frequency, cycles per second

def: A wave whose vibrations are perpendicular to its direction of travel.
ex: Light is a {{transverse wave}} that can be polarised.

def: A wave whose vibrations are parallel to its direction of travel.
ex: Sound travels as a {{longitudinal wave}} through the air.

def: The bouncing back of a wave when it meets a surface.
ex: The {{reflection}} of light in a mirror forms an image.
ph: law of reflection, angle of incidence

def: The bending of a wave as it passes between media of different densities.
ex: {{Refraction}} makes a straw look bent in a glass of water.
ph: bending of light, change of speed

def: The spreading of a wave as it passes through a gap or around an obstacle.
ex: {{Diffraction}} lets sound bend around the edge of a doorway.

def: The combining of two waves to produce a resultant of varying amplitude.
ex: {{Interference}} of light waves creates bright and dark fringes.

def: The large-amplitude vibration when a system is driven at its natural frequency.
ex: {{Resonance}} makes a wine glass shatter at a matching pitch.

def: The full range of electromagnetic waves ordered by frequency and wavelength.
ex: Visible light is a small part of the {{electromagnetic spectrum}}.
ph: range of waves, radio to gamma

def: A band of colours or wavelengths produced by separating light or radiation.
ex: A prism splits white light into a coloured {{spectrum}}.

def: Electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths longer than visible red light.
ex: A remote control sends signals using {{infrared}} radiation.

def: Electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths shorter than visible violet light.
ex: {{Ultraviolet}} radiation from the Sun can damage the skin.

def: High-energy electromagnetic radiation emitted by radioactive nuclei.
ex: A {{gamma ray}} carries far more energy than visible light.

def: High-frequency electromagnetic radiation used to image bones and tissue.
ex: The doctor used an {{x-ray}} to reveal the fractured bone.

def: Electromagnetic waves with the longest wavelengths, used for communication.
ex: A {{radio wave}} carries broadcast signals to your receiver.

def: Electromagnetic radiation between radio waves and infrared in the spectrum.
ex: A {{microwave}} heats food by exciting water molecules.

def: A longitudinal wave of pressure variations that travels through a medium.
ex: A {{sound wave}} cannot travel through empty space.

def: Sound waves with frequencies above the upper limit of human hearing.
ex: Doctors use {{ultrasound}} to produce images of a fetus.

def: How high or low a sound seems, determined by its frequency.
ex: A whistle produces a much higher {{pitch}} than a drum.

def: A sound heard again after being reflected from a distant surface.
ex: The shout produced a clear {{echo}} off the cliff face.

def: A rapid back-and-forth motion of particles about a fixed position.
ex: The string's {{vibration}} produces the note you hear.

def: A surface joining all points of a wave that vibrate in phase.
ex: Each {{wavefront}} of a ripple forms an expanding circle.

def: The stage a wave has reached within its repeating cycle.
ex: Two waves in {{phase}} reinforce each other strongly.

def: Restricting the vibrations of a transverse wave to a single plane.
ex: {{Polarisation}} of light lets sunglasses cut off harsh glare.

def: A wave pattern with fixed nodes formed by two opposing waves.
ex: A {{standing wave}} forms on a plucked guitar string.

def: A point on a standing wave with zero displacement at all times.
ex: At each {{node}} the string does not move at all.
ph: wave node, point of no displacement

def: The distance a wave travels through a medium each second.
ex: The {{wave speed}} equals frequency multiplied by wavelength.
ph: speed of the wave, travels through medium

def: A device storing chemical energy and converting it into electrical energy.
ex: The {{battery}} supplies direct current to the torch.

def: A point where a wire connects to an electrical source or component.
ex: Connect the red wire to the positive {{terminal}} of the cell.

def: A measure of how readily a material lets electric charge flow through it.
ex: Metals have a high electrical {{conductivity}} at room temperature.

def: A current produced in a conductor by a changing magnetic field.
ex: Moving a magnet through a coil creates an {{induced current}}.
ph: induced in coil, changing magnetic field

def: The amount of magnetic flux passing through a unit area.
ex: A stronger magnet gives a higher magnetic {{flux density}}.
ph: magnetic flux density, measured in tesla

def: A brief flash of light caused by electric current jumping a gap.
ex: A {{spark}} leapt across the gap when the charge discharged.

def: The separation of light into its component colours by wavelength.
ex: {{Dispersion}} through a prism creates a band of rainbow colours.

def: A measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance.
ex: The {{temperature}} of the gas rose as it was heated in the container.

def: Energy transferred between bodies because of a difference in temperature.
ex: {{Heat}} flows spontaneously from a hotter object to a colder one.

def: The total kinetic energy of the randomly moving particles within a substance.
ex: The {{thermal energy}} stored in the water increased as it warmed.

def: A measure of the disorder or randomness in a thermodynamic system.
ex: The {{entropy}} of an isolated system always tends to increase over time.

def: The energy needed to raise one kilogram of a substance by one degree.
ex: Water has a high {{specific heat capacity}} compared with most metals.

def: The rate at which a material transfers heat through conduction.
ex: Metals generally have a high {{thermal conductivity}}, so they feel cold.

def: The emission of energy as electromagnetic waves or moving subatomic particles.
ex: Thermal {{radiation}} allows heat to travel through empty space from the Sun.

def: The lowest possible temperature, where particles have minimal kinetic energy.
ex: {{Absolute zero}} corresponds to zero kelvin, or about minus 273 degrees Celsius.

def: The transfer of heat through a material by particle collisions without bulk movement.
ex: Heat travels along the metal spoon by {{conduction}} from the hot end.

def: Heat transfer by the movement of a heated fluid such as air.
ex: Warm air rises by {{convection}}, creating currents that circulate around the room.

def: The total kinetic and potential energy of all particles within a system.
ex: Adding heat to the gas raises its {{internal energy}} and temperature.

def: The branch of physics dealing with heat, work and energy transformations.
ex: The laws of {{thermodynamics}} govern how energy moves between systems.

def: The movement of thermal energy from a hotter region to a cooler one.
ex: {{Heat transfer}} occurs through conduction, convection and radiation.

def: The temperature at which a solid changes into a liquid.
ex: The {{melting point}} of ice is zero degrees Celsius at normal pressure.

def: The temperature at which a liquid rapidly turns into a vapour.
ex: The {{boiling point}} of water is one hundred degrees Celsius at sea level.

def: The energy absorbed or released when a substance changes its physical state.
ex: The {{latent heat}} of vaporisation is needed to boil the liquid completely.

def: The increase in size of a material as its temperature rises.
ex: Thermal {{expansion}} causes the metal bridge to lengthen on hot days.

def: The SI base unit of temperature, starting from absolute zero.
ex: Scientists measure very low temperatures using the {{kelvin}} scale.

def: An instrument used to measure the heat released or absorbed in reactions.
ex: The students used a {{calorimeter}} to find the energy in the fuel.

def: The change of a liquid into a vapour below its boiling point.
ex: {{Evaporation}} of sweat cools the skin on a hot day.

def: The change of a vapour into a liquid as it cools.
ex: {{Condensation}} forms on the cold window when warm air touches it.

def: A device that converts thermal energy into useful mechanical work.
ex: A car engine is a {{heat engine}} that burns fuel to move.

def: The dense central core of an atom containing protons and neutrons.
ex: Almost all of an atom's mass is concentrated in its {{nucleus}}.

def: The positively charged centre of an atom made of nucleons.
ex: The {{atomic nucleus}} is held together by the strong nuclear force.

def: A positively charged subatomic particle found in the atomic nucleus.
ex: The number of {{proton}}s determines which element an atom is.

def: An electrically neutral subatomic particle found in the atomic nucleus.
ex: A {{neutron}} has no charge but almost the same mass as a proton.

def: A particle smaller than an atom, such as a proton or electron.
ex: Protons, neutrons and electrons are the main types of {{subatomic particle}}.

def: An atom of an element with the same protons but different neutrons.
ex: Carbon-14 is a radioactive {{isotope}} used to date ancient remains.

def: The spontaneous emission of radiation from unstable atomic nuclei.
ex: {{Radioactivity}} was first discovered by Henri Becquerel in uranium salts.

def: The process by which an unstable nucleus loses energy by emitting radiation.
ex: {{Radioactive decay}} transforms one element into another over time.

def: The time taken for half the nuclei in a sample to decay.
ex: The {{half-life}} of carbon-14 is about five thousand seven hundred years.

def: A particle of two protons and two neutrons emitted during radioactive decay.
ex: An {{alpha particle}} is easily stopped by a sheet of paper.

def: A high-speed electron emitted from a nucleus during radioactive decay.
ex: A {{beta particle}} can pass through paper but is stopped by aluminium.

def: Radiation with enough energy to remove electrons from atoms it strikes.
ex: Exposure to {{ionising radiation}} can damage living cells and cause cancer.

def: The smallest discrete amount of energy that can be transferred.
ex: A {{quantum}} of light energy is called a photon.

def: The physics describing the behaviour of matter and energy at atomic scales.
ex: {{Quantum mechanics}} explains why electrons occupy fixed energy levels.

def: A quantum of electromagnetic radiation, the particle of light.
ex: Each {{photon}} carries a fixed amount of energy related to its frequency.

def: The concept that matter and light behave as both waves and particles.
ex: {{Wave-particle duality}} is demonstrated by the famous double-slit experiment.

def: Einstein's theory describing space, time and gravity as interrelated.
ex: The theory of {{relativity}} predicts that time slows at high speeds.

def: The splitting of a heavy nucleus into lighter parts, releasing energy.
ex: Nuclear power stations use {{nuclear fission}} of uranium to generate electricity.

def: The joining of light nuclei into a heavier one, releasing huge energy.
ex: The Sun produces its energy through the {{nuclear fusion}} of hydrogen.

def: A self-sustaining series of reactions where each triggers the next.
ex: A nuclear {{chain reaction}} releases energy as neutrons split further nuclei.

def: The energy needed to separate a nucleus into its individual nucleons.
ex: The {{binding energy}} per nucleon is greatest for iron nuclei.

def: The total number of protons and neutrons in an atomic nucleus.
ex: The {{mass number}} of carbon-12 is twelve.

def: The number of protons in the nucleus, defining the element.
ex: Hydrogen has an {{atomic number}} of one because it has one proton.

def: The process of gaining or losing electrons to form charged ions.
ex: {{Ionisation}} occurs when radiation knocks electrons out of atoms.

def: An atom or molecule that has gained or lost electrons.
ex: A sodium atom becomes a positive {{ion}} when it loses an electron.

def: A fixed amount of energy an electron may have within an atom.
ex: An electron jumps to a higher {{energy level}} when it absorbs a photon.

def: The raising of an electron to a higher energy level by absorbing energy.
ex: The {{excitation}} of electrons produces the coloured light in neon signs.

def: The low-level ionising radiation always present in the natural environment.
ex: Rocks and cosmic rays contribute to the {{background radiation}} we all receive.

def: A device that controls nuclear fission to release energy steadily.
ex: Control rods in a {{nuclear reactor}} absorb neutrons to slow the reaction.

def: A process that changes the composition or energy of an atomic nucleus.
ex: A {{nuclear reaction}} can release millions of times more energy than combustion.

def: The principle that mass can be converted into energy and vice versa.
ex: Einstein's equation expresses {{mass-energy equivalence}} as E equals mc squared.

def: The taking in of radiation energy by a material as it passes through.
ex: The {{absorption}} of gamma rays increases with the thickness of lead.

def: The release of radiation or particles from a source of energy.
ex: The {{emission}} of light happens when excited electrons fall back down.

def: The ability of radiation to pass through different materials.
ex: Gamma rays have far greater {{penetrating power}} than alpha particles.

def: A series of radioactive decays leading to a stable nucleus.
ex: Uranium follows a long {{decay chain}} before it finally becomes lead.

def: The rate at which nuclei in a radioactive sample decay per second.
ex: The {{activity}} of the source falls as its unstable nuclei decay away.

def: The random movement of tiny particles suspended in a fluid.
ex: {{Brownian motion}} of pollen grains provided evidence for moving molecules.

def: The force per unit area exerted by gas particles on their container.
ex: The {{gas pressure}} rises when the container is heated at constant volume.

def: A high-energy particle arriving from space and striking the atmosphere.
ex: A {{cosmic ray}} from a distant star contributes to background radiation.

def: The state when two objects in contact reach the same temperature.
ex: The drink and the ice reach {{thermal equilibrium}} at a shared temperature.

def: Temperature measured on the kelvin scale from absolute zero.
ex: The {{absolute temperature}} of the gas is proportional to its pressure.

def: A proton or a neutron, one of the particles making up a nucleus.
ex: The total number of {{nucleon}}s equals the mass number of the atom.

def: A curved transparent element that refracts light to form an image.
ex: The camera uses a glass {{lens}} to focus incoming light.

def: A lens thicker at the centre that converges parallel light rays.
ex: A {{convex lens}} bends parallel rays toward a single focal point.

def: A lens thinner at the centre that spreads parallel light rays apart.
ex: A {{concave lens}} causes light rays to diverge outward.

def: A lens that brings parallel light rays together at a focal point.
ex: A {{converging lens}} focuses sunlight to a hot point.

def: A lens that spreads parallel light rays apart as if from a point.
ex: A {{diverging lens}} always produces a smaller virtual image.

def: The distance between a lens centre and its principal focus point.
ex: A shorter {{focal length}} gives the lens greater bending power.

def: The point where parallel rays converge after passing through a lens.
ex: Rays meet at the {{focal point}} beyond the lens.

def: The plane through the focal point perpendicular to the optical axis.
ex: A sharp image forms on the {{focal plane}} of the lens.

def: The straight line passing through the optical centre and focal points.
ex: The object sits on the {{principal axis}} of the mirror.

def: The point where rays parallel to the axis converge after refraction.
ex: Parallel rays cross at the {{principal focus}} of a lens.

def: The central lens point through which rays pass without deviation.
ex: Light through the {{optical centre}} continues in a straight line.

def: A ratio showing how much a medium slows and bends light.
ex: Diamond has a very high {{refractive index}} of 2.42.

def: Complete reflection of light back into a denser medium beyond critical angle.
ex: Optical fibres transmit signals using {{total internal reflection}} inside glass.

def: The incidence angle above which total internal reflection occurs completely.
ex: Beyond the {{critical angle}}, light cannot escape the water.

def: A law relating the angles and refractive indices of refracted light.
ex: We used {{Snell's law}} to calculate the refraction angle.

def: The angle between an incoming ray and the surface normal line.
ex: The {{angle of incidence}} equals the angle of reflection.

def: The angle between a reflected ray and the surface normal line.
ex: The {{angle of reflection}} matches the angle of incidence exactly.

def: The angle between a refracted ray and the surface normal line.
ex: The {{angle of refraction}} is smaller in a denser medium.

def: The angle between the incident ray and the emergent ray direction.
ex: A prism gives a minimum {{angle of deviation}} at one setting.

def: A ray of light travelling toward a reflecting or refracting surface.
ex: The {{incident ray}} strikes the mirror at a small angle.

def: A ray of light that bounces back from a reflecting surface.
ex: The {{reflected ray}} leaves the surface at an equal angle.

def: A ray of light that bends when passing into another medium.
ex: The {{refracted ray}} bends toward the normal in glass.

def: A transparent block that refracts and separates light into colours.
ex: The glass {{prism}} split white light into a spectrum.

def: An image formed where light rays actually converge on a screen.
ex: A convex lens can project a {{real image}} onto paper.

def: An image that appears where rays only seem to originate.
ex: A plane mirror always forms an upright {{virtual image}}.

def: The ratio of image size to the original object size.
ex: The microscope gives a {{magnification}} of two hundred times.

def: The opening that controls how much light enters an optical system.
ex: A wider {{aperture}} lets more light reach the sensor.

def: A smooth surface that reflects light to form clear images.
ex: A polished {{mirror}} reflects almost all incident light.

def: A flat mirror that forms an upright virtual image.
ex: A {{plane mirror}} shows a laterally inverted reflection of you.

def: A curved mirror that reflects light inward toward a focal point.
ex: A {{concave mirror}} can focus sunlight to a point.

def: A curved mirror that reflects light outward, forming diminished images.
ex: A {{convex mirror}} gives drivers a wide rear view.

def: A mirror whose surface forms part of a sphere.
ex: A {{spherical mirror}} has a definite centre of curvature.

def: A mirror shaped so parallel rays focus at one exact point.
ex: A {{parabolic mirror}} eliminates spherical aberration for parallel beams.

def: A mirror with a curved surface that converges or diverges light.
ex: A {{curved mirror}} changes image size depending on object distance.

def: The centre of the sphere from which a mirror is formed.
ex: Rays through the {{centre of curvature}} reflect straight back.

def: The radius of the sphere defining a curved mirror or lens.
ex: The {{radius of curvature}} equals twice the focal length.

def: A drawing tracing light rays to locate an image.
ex: We drew a {{ray diagram}} to find the image position.

def: The light power emitted by a source in one direction.
ex: The {{luminous intensity}} of the lamp is measured in candela.

def: The luminous flux falling on a surface per unit area.
ex: The desk {{illuminance}} should reach five hundred lux for reading.

def: A device emitting an intense, coherent, narrow beam of light.
ex: The {{laser}} produced a thin beam of red light.

def: A thin glass thread guiding light by total internal reflection.
ex: Data travels through the {{optical fibre}} as pulses of light.

def: The distance from the object to the lens or mirror.
ex: Increasing the {{object distance}} moved the real image closer.

def: The distance from the image to the lens or mirror.
ex: We measured the {{image distance}} to find the focal length.

def: The reciprocal of focal length, measuring a lens's bending strength.
ex: The {{power of a lens}} is greater for shorter focal lengths.

def: The unit of lens power, equal to inverse metres.
ex: His reading glasses have a strength of two {{dioptre}}.

def: Light consisting of a single colour or one wavelength only.
ex: A sodium lamp gives nearly pure {{monochromatic light}}.

def: Light waves that stay in a constant phase relationship together.
ex: A laser emits {{coherent light}} that produces clear interference.

def: Light containing all visible wavelengths mixed together as one.
ex: A prism separates {{white light}} into its rainbow colours.

def: The part of the spectrum that the human eye detects.
ex: The eye detects {{visible light}} between four hundred and seven hundred nanometres.

def: An arc of colours formed by refraction and reflection in raindrops.
ex: A {{rainbow}} appears when sunlight passes through falling raindrops.

def: An instrument using lenses to magnify very small nearby objects.
ex: We viewed the cells through a compound {{microscope}}.

def: An instrument using lenses or mirrors to view distant objects.
ex: The astronomer observed Saturn through a powerful {{telescope}}.

def: A single convex lens used to enlarge nearby small objects.
ex: She read the tiny print with a {{magnifying glass}}.

def: A simple camera forming inverted images through a tiny hole.
ex: A {{pinhole camera}} produces an upside-down image without any lens.

def: The darkest central region of a shadow receiving no direct light.
ex: Within the {{umbra}}, the light source is completely blocked.

def: The lighter outer region of a shadow with partial illumination.
ex: The {{penumbra}} surrounds the umbra during a partial eclipse.

def: The blocking of light when one body passes before another.
ex: A solar {{eclipse}} occurs when the Moon covers the Sun.

def: Not allowing any light to pass through the material.
ex: A brick wall is completely {{opaque}} to visible light.

def: Allowing some light through but scattering it so images blur.
ex: Frosted glass is {{translucent}}, letting light through but blurring shapes.

def: Allowing light to pass through so objects are clearly seen.
ex: Clear window glass is {{transparent}} to visible light.

def: The total visible light power emitted by a source.
ex: The bulb's {{luminous flux}} is rated at eight hundred lumens.

def: The SI unit of illuminance, equal to lumen per square metre.
ex: Bright office lighting provides about five hundred {{lux}}.

def: The SI base unit of luminous intensity in one direction.
ex: The lamp's brightness is stated as ninety {{candela}}.

def: The SI unit of luminous flux emitted by a source.
ex: A modern LED bulb outputs over one thousand {{lumen}}.

def: The perceived intensity of light coming from a source.
ex: Turning the dial increased the screen's {{brightness}} noticeably.

def: An optical illusion from light refraction through hot air layers.
ex: A shimmering {{mirage}} appeared on the hot desert road.

def: The left-right reversal of an image seen in a mirror.
ex: Text shows {{lateral inversion}} when reflected in a plane mirror.

def: A lens defect where colours focus at slightly different points.
ex: Simple lenses show {{chromatic aberration}} as coloured fringes around edges.

def: A defect where outer rays focus differently from central ones.
ex: Wide lenses suffer {{spherical aberration}} that blurs the final image.

def: A surface with fine parallel lines that disperses light spectrally.
ex: A {{diffraction grating}} splits light into a sharp spectrum.

def: The lens nearest the object in a microscope or telescope.
ex: The {{objective lens}} gathers light and forms the first image.

def: The lens near the eye that magnifies the objective's image.
ex: You look through the {{eyepiece}} to see the magnified object.

def: The ability of an instrument to distinguish two close objects.
ex: A larger lens increases the telescope's {{resolving power}}.

def: A collection of light rays travelling in the same direction.
ex: The torch cast a narrow {{beam of light}} across the room.

def: The constant speed light travels in vacuum, about 300000 kilometres per second.
ex: Nothing can travel faster than the {{speed of light}} in vacuum.

def: Force between two point charges varies with product over distance squared.
ex: Using {{Coulomb's law}}, we calculated the repulsion between the charges.

def: Potential energy per unit charge at a point in a field.
ex: The {{electric potential}} decreases with distance from the charge.

def: Line joining points that share the same electric potential value.
ex: No work is done moving a charge along an {{equipotential line}}.

def: Measure of electric field passing through a given surface area.
ex: The {{electric flux}} through the closed surface equals the enclosed charge.

def: Property measuring how a medium resists forming an electric field.
ex: The {{permittivity}} of free space appears in Coulomb's law.

def: Insulating material that increases capacitance when placed between plates.
ex: Inserting a {{dielectric}} raised the capacitor's stored charge.

def: Ability of a component to store charge per unit voltage.
ex: The {{capacitance}} depends on plate area and separation distance.

def: Two parallel conducting plates separated by a small insulating gap.
ex: A {{parallel plate capacitor}} creates a nearly uniform electric field.

def: Rules governing current and voltage in electrical circuit networks.
ex: We solved the network by applying {{Kirchhoff's laws}} to each loop.

def: Resistance within a source that reduces its terminal voltage.
ex: The battery's {{internal resistance}} causes energy loss under load.

def: Average velocity of charge carriers moving through a conductor.
ex: The {{drift velocity}} of electrons in copper is surprisingly slow.

def: Induced current opposes the change in magnetic flux causing it.
ex: According to {{Lenz's law}}, the induced current resists the motion.

def: Property of a coil opposing changes in its own current.
ex: The coil's {{self-inductance}} delays the rise of current.

def: Effect where current in one coil induces voltage in another.
ex: Transformers rely on {{mutual inductance}} between primary and secondary coils.

def: Effective value of an alternating voltage equal to steady equivalent.
ex: The {{root mean square voltage}} of the mains supply is 230 volts.

def: Total opposition to alternating current from resistance and reactance combined.
ex: The circuit's {{impedance}} determines how much current flows.

def: Opposition to alternating current from capacitors or inductors.
ex: Inductive {{reactance}} increases as the supply frequency rises.

def: Force on a charged particle moving in a magnetic field.
ex: The {{Lorentz force}} bends the electron beam into a circle.

def: Pair of equal opposite charges separated by a small distance.
ex: A water molecule behaves as an {{electric dipole}} in fields.

def: Line showing the direction of a field at each point.
ex: The {{field line}} points away from a positive charge.

def: Force per unit positive charge at a point in space.
ex: The {{electric field strength}} between the plates stays uniform.

def: Idealised charge concentrated at a single point in space.
ex: We treat the electron as a {{point charge}} for simplicity.

def: Amount of electric charge per unit length, area, or volume.
ex: The surface {{charge density}} is highest at sharp points.

def: Total electric flux through a surface equals enclosed charge divided.
ex: {{Gauss's law}} simplifies field calculations for symmetric charge distributions.

def: Degree to which a material becomes polarised in an electric field.
ex: High {{electric susceptibility}} means the material polarises strongly.

def: Measure of how easily current flows, the inverse of resistance.
ex: A high {{conductance}} means the wire carries current easily.

def: Material property describing opposition to current independent of dimensions.
ex: Copper has a low {{resistivity}}, making it an excellent conductor.

def: Current is proportional to voltage across a constant resistance.
ex: Using {{Ohm's law}}, we found the current from the voltage.

def: Voltage measured across a source's terminals when supplying current.
ex: The {{terminal voltage}} drops as the battery delivers more current.

def: Particle such as an electron carrying charge through a material.
ex: In metals the main {{charge carrier}} is the free electron.

def: Electric current flowing per unit cross-sectional area of conductor.
ex: A thinner wire has a higher {{current density}} for equal current.

def: Magnetic flux per unit area perpendicular to the field.
ex: The {{magnetic flux density}} near the magnet reaches one tesla.

def: The SI unit of magnetic flux density in the metre-kilogram-second system.
ex: The MRI scanner produces a field of three {{tesla}}.

def: The SI unit measuring total magnetic flux through a surface.
ex: One {{weber}} equals one tesla times one square metre.

def: The SI unit of electrical capacitance equal to coulomb per volt.
ex: A one {{farad}} capacitor stores an enormous amount of charge.

def: The SI unit of inductance in an electrical coil.
ex: The inductor was rated at two {{henry}} of inductance.

def: Wave of oscillating electric and magnetic fields travelling through space.
ex: Light is an {{electromagnetic wave}} that needs no medium.

def: Induced voltage equals the rate of change of magnetic flux.
ex: {{Faraday's law}} explains how generators produce electrical energy.

def: Loop of induced current circulating within a solid conductor.
ex: Transformer cores suffer {{eddy current}} losses that produce heat.

def: Voltage generated by a changing magnetic flux through a coil.
ex: The moving magnet produces an {{induced voltage}} in the coil.

def: Quantity describing the magnetising force produced by a current.
ex: The {{magnetic field strength}} inside the coil depends on turns.

def: Object with two opposite magnetic poles a small distance apart.
ex: A current loop acts as a tiny {{magnetic dipole}}.

def: Unit of magnetic flux density equal to one ten-thousandth tesla.
ex: The Earth's field measures roughly half a {{gauss}}.

def: Nearly uniform magnetic field produced inside a current-carrying coil.
ex: The {{solenoid field}} is strong and uniform along the axis.

def: Measure of how easily a material supports a magnetic field.
ex: Iron's high {{permeability}} concentrates the magnetic field lines.

def: Strong magnetism in materials like iron that retain magnetisation.
ex: The property of {{ferromagnetism}} lets iron become a permanent magnet.

def: Weak magnetism where materials are repelled by a magnetic field.
ex: Weak {{diamagnetism}} causes bismuth to be gently pushed from magnets.

def: Weak attraction of certain materials toward an external magnetic field.
ex: Weak {{paramagnetism}} makes aluminium slightly attracted to strong magnets.

def: Lag between magnetisation and the field that produces it.
ex: Magnetic {{hysteresis}} causes energy loss in the transformer core.

def: Circuit using resistors to produce a fraction of the voltage.
ex: A {{potential divider}} supplies a lower voltage to the sensor.

def: Variable resistor used to control current in a circuit.
ex: Turning the {{rheostat}} dimmed the connected light bulb.

def: Three-terminal resistor providing an adjustable voltage output.
ex: The volume knob is controlled by a {{potentiometer}}.

def: Sensitive instrument that detects and measures small electric currents.
ex: The {{galvanometer}} needle deflected when current flowed through it.

def: Instrument connected in series to measure electric current.
ex: The {{ammeter}} showed a current of two amperes.

def: Instrument connected in parallel to measure potential difference.
ex: The {{voltmeter}} read twelve volts across the battery.

def: Component that allows current to flow in one direction.
ex: The {{diode}} blocks current when connected the wrong way.

def: Diode that emits light when current passes through it.
ex: The indicator uses a red {{light-emitting diode}}.

def: Resistor whose resistance changes strongly with its temperature.
ex: A {{thermistor}} lets the circuit respond to temperature changes.

def: Resistor whose resistance decreases as light intensity increases.
ex: The {{light-dependent resistor}} switches the lamp on at dusk.

def: Study of electric charges that are at rest.
ex: The study of {{electrostatics}} explains why a balloon sticks to walls.

def: Angular difference between voltage and current in an alternating circuit.
ex: The {{phase angle}} shows how much the current lags voltage.

def: Ratio of real power to apparent power in a circuit.
ex: A low {{power factor}} wastes energy in the supply lines.

def: Device converting alternating current into direct current.
ex: The {{rectifier}} turns mains AC into usable DC.

def: Process where a charged capacitor releases its stored charge.
ex: The {{capacitor discharge}} follows an exponential decay curve.

def: Time for a charging or discharging quantity to reach a fraction.
ex: The {{time constant}} equals resistance times capacitance in the circuit.

def: Imaginary part of admittance, the ease of reactive current flow.
ex: The capacitor's {{electric susceptance}} rises with signal frequency.

def: Measure of how easily an alternating current flows, inverse impedance.
ex: The circuit's {{admittance}} is the reciprocal of its impedance.

def: Frequency at which a circuit responds with maximum amplitude.
ex: The radio is tuned to the station's {{resonant frequency}}.

def: Energy a charge possesses due to its position in a field.
ex: Moving the charge closer raises its {{electric potential energy}}.

def: Energy stored within the electric field of a region.
ex: A charged capacitor holds {{electric field energy}} between its plates.

def: Connecting a conductor to the ground for electrical safety.
ex: Proper {{earthing}} protects users from dangerous electric shocks.

def: Unintended low-resistance path allowing excessive current to flow.
ex: A {{short circuit}} caused the fuse to blow instantly.

def: Safety device that melts to break an overloaded circuit.
ex: The {{fuse}} melted when the current became too large.

def: Material that absorbs electromagnetic energy and converts it to heat.
ex: The {{electric susceptor}} heats rapidly under the alternating field.

def: Fundamental force acting between electrically charged particles.
ex: The {{electromagnetic force}} binds electrons to the atomic nucleus.

def: Negative electrode from which conventional current leaves a device.
ex: Electrons are emitted from the heated {{cathode}} in the tube.

def: Positive electrode toward which negative charge carriers move.
ex: The {{anode}} attracts electrons within the vacuum tube.

def: Conductor through which current enters or leaves a medium.
ex: Each {{electrode}} is dipped into the conducting solution.

def: Using electric current to drive a chemical decomposition reaction.
ex: The {{electrolysis}} of water produces hydrogen and oxygen gas.

def: Emission of electrons from a material when light of sufficient frequency strikes it.
ex: Einstein explained the {{photoelectric effect}} using the idea of light quanta.

def: Minimum energy needed to remove an electron from a metal surface.
ex: Each metal has a characteristic {{work function}} measured in electronvolts.

def: Lowest light frequency able to eject electrons from a given metal.
ex: Below the {{threshold frequency}} no electrons are emitted at all.

def: Small unit of energy equal to the work done moving one electron through one volt.
ex: Atomic energies are conveniently expressed in the {{electronvolt}}.

def: Lowest possible energy state of an atom, electron or system.
ex: An unexcited atom normally rests in its {{ground state}}.

def: Higher energy state occupied by an atom after absorbing energy.
ex: The electron jumps to an {{excited state}} when it absorbs a photon.

def: Movement of an electron between two energy levels in an atom.
ex: Each {{electron transition}} emits or absorbs a specific photon energy.

def: Abrupt jump of an electron between discrete atomic energy levels.
ex: A {{quantum leap}} releases energy as a single photon.

def: Set of bright lines produced when excited atoms emit light.
ex: Each element has a unique {{emission spectrum}} of coloured lines.

def: Pattern of dark lines where atoms absorb specific light wavelengths.
ex: The {{absorption spectrum}} shows gaps at characteristic wavelengths.

def: Narrow bright or dark line in a spectrum at one wavelength.
ex: Sodium produces a bright yellow {{spectral line}}.

def: Integer or half-integer labelling an allowed state of a quantum system.
ex: The principal {{quantum number}} determines an electron's energy level.

def: Complete description of a particle's measurable quantum properties.
ex: Measurement forces the system into a definite {{quantum state}}.

def: Position and momentum of a particle cannot both be known exactly.
ex: Heisenberg's {{uncertainty principle}} limits simultaneous measurement precision.

def: Wave associated with a moving particle in quantum theory.
ex: Electrons display {{matter wave}} behaviour in diffraction experiments.

def: Wavelength linked to a particle's momentum by Planck's constant.
ex: A fast electron has a very short {{de Broglie wavelength}}.

def: Mathematical function describing the quantum state of a particle.
ex: The square of the {{wave function}} gives a probability.

def: Likelihood of finding a particle within a region of space.
ex: The {{probability density}} peaks where the electron is most likely.

def: Passage of a particle through a barrier it classically cannot cross.
ex: Alpha particles escape the nucleus through {{quantum tunnelling}}.

def: Quantum state combining several possible states until measurement.
ex: A qubit exists in a {{superposition}} of zero and one.

def: Linked quantum states where measuring one instantly affects the other.
ex: The effect of {{quantum entanglement}} correlates two distant particles.

def: Basic unit of quantum information holding zero and one simultaneously.
ex: A quantum computer stores data in each {{qubit}}.

def: Fundamental constant relating a photon's energy to its frequency.
ex: The {{Planck constant}} sets the scale of quantum effects.
ph: the value of the Planck constant

def: Restriction of a physical quantity to discrete allowed values.
ex: Energy {{quantisation}} explains the discrete lines of atomic spectra.

def: Thermal radiation emitted by an ideal perfectly absorbing object.
ex: Planck explained {{blackbody radiation}} by quantising energy.

def: Increase in photon wavelength after colliding with an electron.
ex: {{Compton scattering}} confirmed the particle nature of X-rays.

def: Creation of a particle and its antiparticle from a photon.
ex: A gamma ray causes {{pair production}} of an electron and positron.

def: Mutual destruction of a particle and antiparticle releasing energy.
ex: Electron-positron {{annihilation}} produces two gamma photons.

def: Matter made of antiparticles having opposite charge to ordinary matter.
ex: When {{antimatter}} meets matter, both are annihilated.

def: Counterpart of a particle with equal mass but opposite charge.
ex: The positron is the {{antiparticle}} of the electron.

def: Positively charged antiparticle of the electron.
ex: Beta-plus decay emits a {{positron}} from the nucleus.

def: Fundamental particle that combines to form protons and neutrons.
ex: A proton is made of three {{quark}} particles.

def: Fundamental particle such as the electron that feels no strong force.
ex: The electron and neutrino are both types of {{lepton}}.

def: Particle with integer spin that carries fundamental forces.
ex: The photon is the {{boson}} mediating electromagnetism.

def: Particle with half-integer spin that obeys the exclusion principle.
ex: Electrons and quarks are examples of a {{fermion}}.

def: Nearly massless neutral particle that rarely interacts with matter.
ex: The Sun emits a huge flux of {{neutrino}} particles.

def: Boson that binds quarks together through the strong force.
ex: A {{gluon}} carries the strong interaction between quarks.

def: Particle whose field gives other fundamental particles their mass.
ex: The {{Higgs boson}} was discovered at CERN in 2012.

def: Heavy unstable lepton resembling a more massive electron.
ex: Cosmic rays produce a {{muon}} in the upper atmosphere.

def: Composite particle made of quarks held by the strong force.
ex: Protons and neutrons are each a type of {{hadron}}.

def: Hadron built from three quarks, such as a proton.
ex: The neutron is a {{baryon}} containing three quarks.

def: Hadron made of one quark and one antiquark.
ex: A pion is a light {{meson}} in particle physics.

def: Intrinsic angular momentum carried by a fundamental particle.
ex: Every electron has a {{spin}} of one half.

def: Theory describing fundamental particles and three of their forces.
ex: The {{standard model}} classifies quarks, leptons and bosons.

def: Machine that speeds charged particles to very high energies.
ex: The {{particle accelerator}} collides protons at near light speed.

def: Accelerator that spins charged particles in a spiral using magnetic fields.
ex: The {{cyclotron}} accelerates protons along a widening spiral path.

def: Ring accelerator using synchronised magnetic and electric fields.
ex: A {{synchrotron}} produces intense beams of X-rays.

def: High-energy impact between particles used to study their structure.
ex: A {{particle collision}} can create many new short-lived particles.

def: Device revealing charged particle tracks as trails of condensation.
ex: An alpha particle leaves a thick track in the {{cloud chamber}}.

def: Flash of light produced when radiation strikes certain materials.
ex: A detector counts each {{scintillation}} caused by a particle.

def: Instrument that detects and counts ionising radiation events.
ex: The {{Geiger counter}} clicked faster near the source.

def: Number of radiation events detected per unit of time.
ex: The {{count rate}} halves after each half-life passes.

def: Force binding quarks and holding the atomic nucleus together.
ex: The {{strong nuclear force}} overcomes proton repulsion in the nucleus.

def: Force responsible for beta decay and changing quark types.
ex: The {{weak nuclear force}} governs radioactive beta decay.

def: One of the four basic interactions governing all matter.
ex: Gravity is the weakest {{fundamental force}} in nature.

def: Particle with no known internal structure or components.
ex: The electron is regarded as an {{elementary particle}}.

def: Region around a nucleus where electrons are likely found.
ex: The {{electron cloud}} replaces fixed orbits in quantum theory.

def: Group of atomic orbitals sharing a similar energy level.
ex: The outer {{electron shell}} determines chemical behaviour.

def: Region describing the probable location of an electron in an atom.
ex: Each {{atomic orbital}} holds a maximum of two electrons.

def: Outermost electron of an atom involved in bonding.
ex: A metal readily loses its single {{valence electron}}.

def: No two identical fermions can occupy the same quantum state.
ex: Pauli's {{exclusion principle}} explains atomic electron arrangements.

def: Smallest discrete packet of energy a system can gain or lose.
ex: Light delivers energy in each {{energy quantum}} called a photon.

def: Average energy holding each nucleon within a nucleus.
ex: Iron has the highest {{binding energy per nucleon}}.
ph: a graph of binding energy per nucleon

def: Difference between a nucleus mass and its separate nucleons.
ex: The {{mass defect}} converts into nuclear binding energy.

def: Unstable isotope that emits radiation as it decays.
ex: A medical {{radioisotope}} is used as a tracer.

def: Probability per unit time that a given nucleus decays.
ex: A large {{decay constant}} means rapid radioactive decay.

def: Atomic species defined by its numbers of protons and neutrons.
ex: Each {{nuclide}} is written with its mass and atomic number.

def: Radioactive decay emitting an electron or positron from the nucleus.
ex: During {{beta decay}} a neutron changes into a proton.

def: Radioactive decay releasing a helium nucleus from the atom.
ex: Uranium loses two protons through {{alpha decay}}.

def: Emission of a high-energy photon from an excited nucleus.
ex: After {{gamma decay}} the nucleus reaches a lower energy state.

def: Change of one element into another through nuclear reactions.
ex: Radioactive decay causes the {{transmutation}} of one element.

def: Minimum amount of fissile material needed for a chain reaction.
ex: A sustained reaction begins once the {{critical mass}} is reached.

def: Substance whose nuclei can sustain a fission chain reaction.
ex: Uranium-235 is a common {{fissile material}}.

def: Material that slows neutrons to sustain a fission reaction.
ex: Water acts as a {{moderator}} inside many reactors.

def: Rod that absorbs neutrons to control a reactor's reaction rate.
ex: Lowering a {{control rod}} slows the chain reaction.

def: Radioactive substance followed through a system to study processes.
ex: Doctors inject a {{tracer}} to image blood flow.

def: Ability of radiation to knock electrons from atoms it passes.
ex: Alpha particles have the greatest {{ionising power}}.

def: Device that produces and accelerates a narrow beam of electrons.
ex: An {{electron gun}} fires electrons at the screen.

def: Release of electrons from a hot metal surface.
ex: A hot filament releases electrons by {{thermionic emission}}.

def: Stream of electrons emitted from a cathode in a vacuum tube.
ex: A magnet deflects the beam of a {{cathode ray}}.

def: Principle that wave and particle descriptions are both needed.
ex: Bohr's {{complementarity}} unites wave and particle pictures.

def: Stable atomic energy state in which no radiation is emitted.
ex: An electron in a {{stationary state}} does not lose energy.

def: Energy required to remove an electron from an atom.
ex: The {{ionisation energy}} rises across a period in the table.

def: A luminous sphere of plasma held together by its own gravity, emitting light.
ex: The Sun is the nearest {{star}} to Earth.

def: A large body orbiting a star, massive enough to be rounded by its own gravity.
ex: Jupiter is the largest {{planet}} in the Solar System.

def: A vast system of stars, gas and dust bound together by gravity.
ex: The Milky Way is a spiral {{galaxy}} containing billions of stars.

def: The Sun together with the planets and other bodies orbiting around it.
ex: There are eight major planets in the {{solar system}}.

def: An icy body that releases gas and dust, forming a glowing tail near the Sun.
ex: Halley's {{comet}} returns to the inner Solar System roughly every 76 years.

def: A small rocky body orbiting the Sun, mainly between Mars and Jupiter.
ex: A large {{asteroid}} may have caused the extinction of the dinosaurs.

def: The streak of light produced when a small body burns up in the atmosphere.
ex: We watched a bright {{meteor}} flash across the night sky.

def: A fragment of rock or metal that survives and lands on a planet's surface.
ex: The iron {{meteorite}} was found buried deep in the desert sand.

def: A natural satellite that orbits a planet or other larger body.
ex: The {{moon}} controls the ocean tides through its gravitational pull.

def: A body or device that orbits a planet, star or other larger object.
ex: A weather {{satellite}} orbits Earth to track approaching storms.

def: A region where gravity is so strong that not even light can escape.
ex: A supermassive {{black hole}} sits at the centre of our galaxy.

def: A powerful explosion marking the violent death of a massive star.
ex: A {{supernova}} can briefly outshine an entire galaxy of stars.

def: An interstellar cloud of gas and dust, often where new stars form.
ex: The Orion {{nebula}} is a bright stellar nursery visible with binoculars.

def: A large, cool, luminous star in a late stage of its evolution.
ex: Our Sun will swell into a {{red giant}} in about five billion years.

def: The dense, cooling remnant core left behind by a dying low-mass star.
ex: A {{white dwarf}} is roughly the size of Earth but extremely dense.

def: An extremely dense collapsed core composed almost entirely of neutrons.
ex: A teaspoon of {{neutron star}} material would weigh billions of tonnes.

def: A rotating neutron star emitting regular beams of electromagnetic radiation.
ex: Astronomers detect a {{pulsar}} by its steady pulses of radio waves.

def: An extremely luminous galactic core powered by a supermassive black hole.
ex: A distant {{quasar}} can outshine the combined light of its host galaxy.

def: The distance light travels in one year, used to measure space distances.
ex: The nearest star is over four {{light year}}s away from Earth.

def: A unit of astronomical distance equal to about 3.26 light years.
ex: One {{parsec}} is defined using the parallax angle of a star.

def: The average distance between the Earth and the Sun.
ex: Mars orbits at roughly 1.5 {{astronomical unit}}s from the Sun.

def: The lengthening of light waves from objects moving away from us.
ex: The {{redshift}} of distant galaxies shows the universe is expanding.

def: The shortening of light waves from objects moving towards the observer.
ex: The {{blueshift}} of the Andromeda galaxy shows it is approaching us.

def: The change in wave frequency caused by relative motion of source and observer.
ex: Astronomers use the {{Doppler shift}} to measure how fast a star moves.

def: Faint radiation left over from the early hot phase of the universe.
ex: The {{cosmic microwave background}} is strong evidence for the Big Bang.

def: The theory that the universe began from an extremely hot, dense state.
ex: The {{Big Bang}} occurred roughly 13.8 billion years ago.

def: The observation that galaxies are moving apart as space itself stretches.
ex: Hubble's measurements first revealed an {{expanding universe}}.

def: Invisible matter detected only by its gravitational effect on galaxies.
ex: Most of the mass in a galaxy appears to be {{dark matter}}.

def: A mysterious force thought to drive the accelerating expansion of the universe.
ex: {{Dark energy}} makes up about seventy per cent of the universe.

def: The minimum speed needed to break free from a body's gravitational pull.
ex: A rocket must reach {{escape velocity}} to leave Earth's gravity.

def: The bending of light by a massive object lying along the line of sight.
ex: {{Gravitational lensing}} lets astronomers see galaxies hidden behind others.

def: The total amount of energy a star radiates each second.
ex: A star's {{luminosity}} depends strongly on its mass and temperature.

def: A measure of how bright a star appears from Earth.
ex: The lower the {{apparent magnitude}}, the brighter the star looks.

def: A recognisable pattern of stars as seen from Earth.
ex: Orion is one of the most easily recognised {{constellation}}s.

def: The scientific study of the origin, structure and evolution of the universe.
ex: Modern {{cosmology}} describes how the universe grew from the Big Bang.

def: The science that studies celestial objects and phenomena beyond Earth.
ex: She studied {{astronomy}} to learn how stars are born and die.

def: The branch of physics dealing with the nature of celestial bodies.
ex: {{Astrophysics}} explains the processes powering the interiors of stars.

def: A building equipped with telescopes for observing the sky and space.
ex: The mountaintop {{observatory}} offers clear views of the night sky.

def: A natural object located in outer space, such as a star or planet.
ex: The Moon is the closest {{celestial body}} to our planet.

def: Tiny solid particles floating in the space between the stars.
ex: New planets form from grains of {{cosmic dust}} around young stars.

def: The gas and dust filling the space between stars in a galaxy.
ex: Radiation from stars heats the surrounding {{interstellar medium}}.

def: The sequence of changes a star undergoes over its lifetime.
ex: {{Stellar evolution}} describes how a star ages from birth to death.

def: The long stable phase in which a star fuses hydrogen in its core.
ex: The Sun is currently a stable {{main sequence}} star.

def: The boundary around a black hole beyond which nothing can escape.
ex: Once past the {{event horizon}}, not even light can return.

def: A point of infinite density thought to lie at a black hole's centre.
ex: General relativity predicts a {{singularity}} inside every black hole.

def: A stream of charged particles flowing outward from the Sun.
ex: The {{solar wind}} shapes the tails of passing comets.

def: A sudden, intense burst of radiation from the Sun's surface.
ex: A strong {{solar flare}} can disrupt radio communication on Earth.

def: A cooler, darker region on the Sun's surface caused by magnetic activity.
ex: The number of {{sunspot}}s rises and falls in an eleven-year cycle.

def: An event where the Moon passes between the Sun and Earth, blocking light.
ex: During a total {{solar eclipse}}, day briefly turns to darkness.

def: An event in which Earth's shadow falls upon the Moon.
ex: A {{lunar eclipse}} can give the Moon a deep reddish colour.

def: The changing shape of the Moon's lit part as seen from Earth.
ex: The new moon is the darkest {{phase of the moon}}.

def: The regular rise and fall of sea level caused by gravitational pull.
ex: The Moon's gravity is the main cause of ocean {{tide}}s.

def: An imaginary line through a body about which it rotates.
ex: Earth spins once a day around its tilted {{axis}}.

def: The spinning of a body around its own internal axis.
ex: Earth's {{rotation}} produces the cycle of day and night.

def: The motion of one body travelling in a complete path around another.
ex: Earth completes one {{revolution}} around the Sun each year.

def: A cascade of particles produced when cosmic rays strike the atmosphere.
ex: Detectors on the ground record each {{cosmic ray shower}} from space.

def: The dense central region around which a galaxy rotates.
ex: A supermassive black hole lies at the {{galactic centre}}.

def: A curved region of stars and gas winding out from a galaxy's centre.
ex: Our Sun lies within a {{spiral arm}} of the Milky Way.

def: A group of stars or galaxies held together by mutual gravity.
ex: A globular {{cluster}} may contain hundreds of thousands of stars.

def: The rate at which the universe expands with increasing distance.
ex: Measuring the {{Hubble constant}} tells us the age of the universe.

def: The enormous range of sizes and distances found across the universe.
ex: On a {{cosmic scale}}, entire galaxies appear as tiny points.

def: The near-empty space between celestial bodies, almost free of matter.
ex: Sound cannot travel through the {{vacuum of space}}.

def: The time a body takes to complete one full orbit around another.
ex: Earth's {{orbital period}} around the Sun is one year.

def: The point in an orbit closest to the Sun.
ex: A comet moves fastest as it passes through {{perihelion}}.

def: The point in an orbit farthest from the Sun.
ex: At {{aphelion}}, a planet travels more slowly along its path.

def: The inward fall of matter under its own gravity, forming dense objects.
ex: {{Gravitational collapse}} of a massive star can create a black hole.

def: The joining of light nuclei in stellar cores, releasing vast energy.
ex: {{Nuclear fusion in stars}} converts hydrogen into helium and light.

def: A planet that orbits a star outside our own Solar System.
ex: Thousands of an {{exoplanet}} have been discovered orbiting distant stars.

def: The region around a star where liquid water could exist on a planet.
ex: An Earth-like planet in the {{habitable zone}} might support life.

def: A roughly spherical region of stars and dark matter around a galaxy.
ex: The {{galactic halo}} contains old stars and unseen dark matter.

def: The ongoing stretching of space that carries galaxies apart.
ex: {{Cosmic expansion}} is accelerating due to dark energy.

def: An unmanned spacecraft sent to explore distant bodies in space.
ex: A {{space probe}} sent back close-up images of Saturn's rings.

def: A vehicle designed to travel and operate in outer space.
ex: The {{spacecraft}} entered orbit around Mars after a long journey.

def: A large group of galaxies bound together by gravity.
ex: A single {{galaxy cluster}} can contain thousands of galaxies.

def: A ripple in spacetime produced by accelerating massive objects.
ex: A {{gravitational wave}} was detected from two merging black holes.

def: The four-dimensional framework combining space and time in relativity.
ex: Massive objects bend the fabric of {{spacetime}} around them.

def: A rounded body orbiting the Sun that has not cleared its orbital path.
ex: Pluto was reclassified as a {{dwarf planet}} in 2006.

def: A bowl-shaped hollow formed by an impact or volcanic activity.
ex: The Moon's surface is covered with impact {{crater}}s.

def: An imaginary sphere on which all sky objects appear projected.
ex: Stars seem fixed to the rotating {{celestial sphere}} above us.

def: A set of planets and bodies orbiting one or more central stars.
ex: Astronomers have found many a {{planetary system}} beyond our own.
